summary: Spolsky addresses the growing spam problem and the collateral damage caused by overzealous spam filtering. He describes how aggressive filters are bouncing legitimate emails, including his own double-opt-in mailing list, because the delivery provider's IP was blacklisted. He advocates for tagging-based spam filters over bounce-based ones, since bouncing risks losing wanted mail. He then proposes a micropayment system where sending an email costs one cent, which would be economically prohibitive for spammers but trivial for legitimate senders.
key_points: Aggressive spam filters that bounce rather than tag emails cause collateral damage by blocking legitimate mail
Even double-opt-in mailing lists operated by reputable providers are getting caught in IP-based blacklists
Tagging spam and letting users sort it is far safer than bouncing it before delivery
A micropayment system (one cent per email) would make spam economically unviable while remaining trivial for legitimate senders
The micropayment model could even redistribute most of the cost to recipients as sending credits, creating a self-sustaining system
money_quotes: My incoming spam ratio is well over 50% by now.
Overzealous system administrators are causing serious damage to the connectivity of the Internet by imposing draconian spam filters.
If you or your ISP is using a spam filter that bounces mail, you're going to lose stuff that you didn't want to lose.
No spammer can afford the penny times the 19 million messages they send.
Eventually, if it caught on, you wouldn't need a spam filter: just put all the free email in a suspect folder, and check it once a week.
key_insight: The real damage from spam isn't the spam itself but the aggressive filtering systems that block legitimate communication — the solution is economic disincentives rather than blunt technical barriers.
